coredata.dc:title: Interfacial properties, decompression, and augmented injection of amphiphilic carbon dots and surfactant mixtures
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shiwei
Lu, Renling
Wang, Gang
Liu, Huarong
Lai, Lu
coredata.dc:description: The mixing properties of amphiphilic carbon dots named C12-SCDs with a cationic surfactant (Dodecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, DTAB) and an anionic surfactant (Sodium dodecyl sulfate, SDS) were investigated. Results indicated that surfactants DTAB and SDS show a synergistic effect in reducing surface tension with C12-SCDs. The synergistic effect of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture is more prominent than the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture, which may result from the electrostatic attraction between DTAB and C12-SCDs. Meanwhile, adding inorganic salts, the surface tension of these mixtures shows a trend of first decreasing slightly and then increasing. The DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture resists strong acids but not alkali environments. However, the surface tension of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture gradually increases with the increase in pH. In addition, these mixtures have temperature-resistance properties. C12-SCDs and surfactants (DTAB and SDS) also have a synergistic effect in reducing the crude oil-water interfacial tension. Meanwhile, the DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures have excellent wettability alternation performance. C12-SCDs and surfactants can form a mixed adsorption layer to promote the wettability alternation performance. The depressurization rates of DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/ C12-SCDs mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs mixture are 10.42 %, 11.54 %, 22.91 %, 26.32 %, and 24.75 %, respectively. The DTAB/C12-CDs and SDS/C12-CDs mixtures can effectively remove the oil film on the core surface, thus improving the channel of injected water. At the same time, these mixtures can be adsorbed on the surface of the hydrophilic core to form a hydrophobic surface, and the injection of water produces a hydrophobic slip effect. Finally, reducing the surface tension of water can also effectively reduce the injection pressure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots
Surfactant
Interfacial properties
Decompression
Augmented injection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of crude oil-water interfacial tension
Measurements of decompression and augmented injection
Equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
Effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
Effects of pH on the equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
Effects of temperature on the equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
Crude oil-water interfacial properties of CDs and surfactant mixtures
Wettability alternation and oil film removal properties of CDs and surfactant mixtures
Decompression and augmented injection of CDs and surfactant mixtures

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Equilibrium surface tension curves of C12-SCDs, DTAB, SDS, DTAB/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture at 25.0 °C. The number in the brackets is a mass ratio.
Variation of the surface tension of DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures with the mixing ratios at different total concentrations and 25.0 °C.
Photographs of the DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures with different mass ratios at 25.0 °C and c total= 400 mg/L.

Effects of NaCl and CaCl2 concentration on the surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) (a, b) and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures (5:5) (c, d) with different concentrations (50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, and 200 mg/L) and 25.0 °C.
Effects of pH values on the surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) (a) and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) (b) mixtures with different concentrations (50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, and 200 mg/L) at 25.0 °C.
The crude oil-water interfacial tension curves of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) (a) and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) (b) mixtures at 60.0 °C. The total concentration of each sample is 500 mg/L.

The underwater contact angle (θ) (a) and photograph (b) of oil droplets on the glass sheets immersing in deionized water, DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture. The total concentration of each sample is 500 mg/L.

(a) Photographs of oil film removal by DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7), and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixtures at different treatment times; (b) The relative area of residual oil film treated by DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture at different treatment times. The total concentration of each sample is 500 mg/L.
Variation of injection pressure of DTAB (a), SDS (a), C12-SCDs (a), DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture (b), and SDS/C12-SCDs(5:5) mixture (c). The CDs or surfactant concentration is 200 mg/L.
Apparent CMC and apparent γ CMC of C12-SCDs, DTAB, SDS, DTAB/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture at 25.0 °C.

head.ce:title: Interfacial properties, decompression, and augmented injection of amphiphilic carbon dots and surfactant mixtures

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Yangtze University, Jingzhou 434023, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The mixing properties of amphiphilic carbon dots named C12-SCDs with a cationic surfactant (Dodecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide, DTAB) and an anionic surfactant (Sodium dodecyl sulfate, SDS) were investigated. Results indicated that surfactants DTAB and SDS show a synergistic effect in reducing surface tension with C12-SCDs. The synergistic effect of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture is more prominent than the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture, which may result from the electrostatic attraction between DTAB and C12-SCDs. Meanwhile, adding inorganic salts, the surface tension of these mixtures shows a trend of first decreasing slightly and then increasing. The DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture resists strong acids but not alkali environments. However, the surface tension of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture gradually increases with the increase in pH. In addition, these mixtures have temperature-resistance properties. C12-SCDs and surfactants (DTAB and SDS) also have a synergistic effect in reducing the crude oil-water interfacial tension. Meanwhile, the DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures have excellent wettability alternation performance. C12-SCDs and surfactants can form a mixed adsorption layer to promote the wettability alternation performance. The depressurization rates of DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/ C12-SCDs mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs mixture are 10.42 %, 11.54 %, 22.91 %, 26.32 %, and 24.75 %, respectively. The DTAB/C12-CDs and SDS/C12-CDs mixtures can effectively remove the oil film on the core surface, thus improving the channel of injected water. At the same time, these mixtures can be adsorbed on the surface of the hydrophilic core to form a hydrophobic surface, and the injection of water produces a hydrophobic slip effect. Finally, reducing the surface tension of water can also effectively reduce the injection pressure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanotechnology has been widely used in exploring oil and gas fields, drilling and completion, and enhanced oil recovery (EOR) [1,2]. Based on the adsorption, desorption, and migration of nanoparticles at the three-phase interface of oil, water, and rock, the researchers proposed different EOR mechanisms of nanomaterials, such as wedge-shaped extrusion, wetting reversal, plugging, reducing interfacial tension, and regulating viscosity [3]. The reported oil-displacement nanomaterials mainly include metal oxide nanomaterials, such as Al2O3, ZrO2, TiO2, Fe3O4, and MgO [4,5]; nano-SiO2 [6]; polymer nanospheres [7]; carbon nanomaterials, such as nano-graphite, carbon nanofibers, graphene, carbon nanoparticles [8]. However, most oil-displacement nanomaterials have a large particle size, no amphiphilicity, and low interfacial activity. In order to obtain amphiphilicity, it is often necessary to use surface modifiers to modify the surface of nanomaterials, which will inevitably lead to problems such as increased costs and material stability. In addition to the nanomaterials mentioned above, amphiphilic Janus nanoparticles have also attracted much attention as an asymmetric material [9,10]. For example, Luo et al. from the University of Houston prepared graphene-based Janus nanosheets, which can spontaneously adsorb at the oil-water interface and increase oil recovery by 15.2 % [11]. Although the thickness of these Janus nanosheets is 1 nm, the average hydrated particle size is 120.6 ± 6.8 nm, so these nanosheets are large for the nanoscale pore throats of low-permeability reservoirs. At the same time, the preparation of Janus nanoparticles is relatively cumbersome, and the application cost is higher than that of surfactants. In summary, the low-cost preparation of ultra-small size and high interfacial activity oil-displacement nanomaterials is still the technical bottleneck for applying nanotechnology in the EOR [12].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of carbon nanomaterials with a 1–10 nm size and the ability to emit fluorescence. Due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and luminescent stability, CDs have broad application prospects in bioimaging, fluorescence sensing, and optoelectronic energy [13]. At the same time, CDs have also been used in EOR research. For example, Zhao et al. used petroleum coke as a carbon source to prepare CDs by electrochemical methods and mixed the above CDs with Tween-80 to prepare carbon nanofluids after freeze-drying [14]. The results of imbibition experiments show that the oil recovery rate of the nanofluid is 24 %, higher than 11 % of the sodium chloride aqueous solution. Wu et al. also used petroleum coke as a carbon source to prepare strongly hydrophilic CDs by combining electrochemical and ozonation methods [15]. The above CDs can be used for depressurization, enhanced injection, and enhanced oil recovery. They attributed the above properties to the change in wettability caused by the adsorption of CDs on the rock surface. Wu et al. used glucose and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane as raw materials to prepare amine-modified hydrophilic CDs [16]. The oil recovery rate of the CDs is as high as 54.09 %, which is higher than that of water flooding (30.25 %), nano-silica (36.45 %), and CDs without amine group (36.45 %). Baragau et al. prepared sulfur-functionalized carbon dots by continuous supercritical hydrothermal synthesis using glucose and p-sulfocalix[4]arene as raw materials. The reaction temperature and pressure were 450 °C and 24.8 MPa, respectively. When the mass percentage is 0.01 %, the CDs can increase the recovery by 17 % [17]. In addition, the fluorescence properties of CDs have also been used in oilfield tracing research. For example, Ranjbar et al. synthesized CDs by microwave method using citric acid and thiourea as raw materials [18]. Using phenylenediamine and tartaric acid as raw materials, they synthesized green and yellow fluorescent CDs by solvothermal method [19]. The above CDs prepare nanofluids for oilfield tracing and enhanced oil recovery. However, the CDs reported above usually have strong hydrophilicity but not amphiphilicity and the surface activity is not high.

Amphiphilic CDs can be dispersed in both aqueous and organic phases and have unique application prospects compared with hydrophilic or hydrophobic CDs that can only be dispersed in aqueous or organic phases. In 2012, Wang et al. prepared amphiphilic CDs from eggs, which were soluble in water and organic solvents [20]. Kozak et al. used cetylpyridinium chloride as a carbon source to prepare amphiphilic CDs by a hydrothermal method [21]. The fluorescence emission of the above CDs was adjustable in the range of blue-green-yellow light. Since then, amphiphilic CDs have been used in the fluorescence imaging of cell membranes [22], bacterial detection [23], ion detection [24], and drug carriers [25]. So far, the relevant researches mainly focus on the optical properties of amphiphilic CDs in different solvents, the biomarkers and transmembrane properties of amphiphilic CDs, etc., and there are few studies on the surface and interfacial activities of amphiphilic CDs. Our group also prepared a series of amphiphilic CDs using amines and citric acid with different carbon chain lengths as raw materials [26]. The synthesized C12-CDs and C12-SCDs have ultra-small size (1 nm∼4 nm), the critical micelle concentration is 100 mg/L and 200 mg/L, respectively, and the surface tension at the critical micelle concentration is 27.45 mN/m and 28.41 mN/m, the aforementioned amphiphilic CDs exhibit surfactant-like properties. In this study, the mixing properties of amphiphilic CDs (C12-SCDs) with one cationic surfactant (DTAB) and one anionic surfactant (SDS) were investigated. The surface/interfacial properties of these mixtures have been carefully studied, and we believe that these results will benefit the large-scale application of amphiphilic CDs.

Amphiphilic CDs (C12-SCDs) were synthesized from citric acid, sulfamic acid, and dodecyl amine (C12H25NH2) by ourselves, and the detailed synthesize route and characterization of C12-SCDs are shown in reference [26]. Specifically, citric acid (20.0 g) and dodecyl amine (20.0 g) were added to 250 mL of three-neck flasks and heated at 180 °C for three hours in the oil bath. The obtained products were then dissolved in absolute ethanol (200 mL). Next, the CDs solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the undissolved substances. After removing ethanol by reduced pressure distillation, the CDs were dissolved in ultrapure water and dialyzed with a 1 kDa dialysis membrane for 12 h to remove small molecules. Finally, the CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. To further sulfonated CDs, CDs (10.0 g) and sulfamic acid (10.0 g) were added to methanol (50 mL) and refluxed at 70 °C for five hours. After removing methanol by reduced pressure distillation, the dried crude product was dissolved in ethanol, filtered, and dried to obtain sulfonated amphiphilic CDs (C12-SCDs). Before each experiment, the C12-SCDs solution was dialyzed with a 1 kDa dialysis membrane for 48 h to ensure no small molecules with surface activity were present.

Dodecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (DTAB) and sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China). After receiving, DTAB was recrystallized three times from the mixture of acetone and absolute alcohol (v/v=3:1). SDS was washed twice with diethyl ether to remove trace dodecanol, then recrystallized twice from the ethanol/water mixture (v/v=5:1). All other reagents were of analytical grade and the experimental water was deionized water with the electrical conductivity less than 10 μS∙cm−1. Crude oil was obtained from an oilfield of China National Offshore Oil Corporation (China). The properties and components of crude oil are shown in Table S1.

A K11 automatic tensiometer (Krüss, German) using the platinum ring method measured the equilibrium surface tension curves of individual or mixed surfactants. Before measurement, the tensiometer was calibrated using deionized water and absolute ethyl alcohol. First, the surface tension was determined at 25.0 °C ± 0.1 °C until a constant value was attained. Then, each measurement was repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of crude oil-water interfacial tension
ce:section.ce:para: The interfacial tension (IFT) between surfactants (or CDs) aqueous solution and crude oil was determined using a TX-500 spinning drop interfacial tensiometer (Zhongchen, China). First, the aqueous solution was injected into the glass tube. Then, a droplet of crude oil (10 μL) was injected into the aqueous solution. Finally, the IFT was measured at 6000 rpm and 60.0 °C ± 0.1 °C.

First, the glass sheets were immersed in a piranha solution (concentrated sulfuric acid (98 %) and hydrogen peroxide (30 %) with a volume ratio of 3:1) to remove impurities. Next, the glass sheets were washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C. A DSA30 contact angle measuring instrument (Krüss, German) was used to observe the underwater contact angle (θ) of oil droplets on the hydrophilic glass sheets immersing in deionized water, surfactant, CDs, and surfactant/CDs solution.

The lipophilic treatment of glass sheets was prior to a contact angle measurement. First, the glass sheets were immersed in a piranha solution (concentrated sulfuric acid (98 %) and hydrogen peroxide (30 %) with a volume ratio of 3:1) to remove impurities. Next, the glass sheets were washed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C. Finally, the glass sheets were immersed in dimethyl silicone oil and aged at 60 °C for 48 h to obtain oil-wet glass sheets.

After lipophilic treatment, the surface of the glass sheets was coated with crude oil and aged at 100 °C for 48 h to simulate the oil film on the rock surface of the reservoir. The glass sheets were immersed in surfactant and CDs solutions, respectively, and the oil film removal was recorded at different treatment times. The relative area of residual oil film at different times was obtained by the graphical analysis software.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of decompression and augmented injection
ce:section.ce:para: The core flow experiment was used to simulate the water injection development process in the oil field. The depressurization and injection enhancement performance of DTAB, SDS, C12-CDs, and their mixed solutions were studied, respectively. First, the mineralized water (total dissolved solid=10,000 mg/L) was injected, and the permeability and pore volume of the core was measured at a constant flow rate of 2.0 mL/min. After drying at 120 °C for 6 h, the core was saturated with crude oil and then put into the core holder. The mineralized water was injected into the core at a low flow rate until the pressure equalized and no more oil was released. Next, the above cores were dried, and after the residual crude oil was entirely aged, different solutions were injected into the cores at a flow rate of 1.0 mL/min. The corresponding displacement pressures were recorded, and the depressurization rate was calculated. The cores used in this experiment are artificial sandstone cores.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were first synthesized from citric acid and dodecyl amine at 180 °C for three hours, then reacted with sulfamic acid at 70 °C for five hours to obtain C12-SCDs containing sulfonic acid groups finally. According to the synthetic raw materials and the characterization results in Figs. S1-S4 and Table S2, the surface of C12-SCDs mainly consists of long-chain alkyl, hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate groups. Specific characterization analyses were provided in Supporting Information.

As shown in Fig. S5, the isoelectric points of C12-SCDs are 3.1. The zeta potential of C12-SCDs decreases with the increase in pH, resulting from the ionization degree change of the hydroxyl, carboxyl, amine, and sulfonate groups on the C12-SCDs surface. According to our measurement, ζ equals − 9.8 mV at pH= 7.0 [26]. Therefore, C12-SCDs and SDS are negative at pH= 7.0, while DTAB is positive at the same pH value. As shown in Fig. 1, the surface tension curves of C12-SCDs, DTAB, SDS, and their mixtures were measured using the platinum ring method. The amphiphilic CDs (C12-SCDs) exhibit a surface tension curve similar to that of surfactants, which is related to the amphiphilic nature of these C12-SCDs. Because there are hydrophilic carboxyl, amine, hydroxyl, and sulfonic acid groups on the surface of CDs, and hydrophobic alkyl chains, therefore, these CDs, like surfactants, can also generate surface adsorption to reduce the surface tension of the solution. According to Fig. 1, the apparent critical micelle concentration (apparent CMC) and the surface tension at apparent CMC (apparent γ CMC) of these CDs, surfactants, and mixtures were obtained and listed in Table 1.

It can be seen from Table 1 that the CMC of DTAB, SDS, and the apparent CMC of C12-SCDs are 4080, 880, and 150 mg/L, respectively. The apparent CMC of C12-SCDs is lower than the CMC of single-chain surfactants DTAB and SDS. Meanwhile, the apparent γ CMC of C12-SCDs is lower than that of DTAB and SDS. After mixing C12-SCDs with the above surfactants (DTAB and SDS), the apparent CMC and apparent γ CMC of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (5:5) and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixtures are lower than those of the two pure components. Surfactants DTAB and SDS show a synergistic effect in reducing surface tension with C12-SCDs. Among these mixtures, the decrease in apparent CMC and apparent γ CMC of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture is more prominent. At the DTAB/C12-SCDs mass ratio of 5:5, apparent γ CMC reaches the lowest at 24.88 mN/m.

In order to further study the effects of mass ratio on the surface activity of these mixtures, various mixtures with different mass ratios were prepared at fixed concentrations, and the surface tension values of these mixtures were measured. As shown in Fig. 2, these surface tension curves of the above mixtures are all concave. When the mass ratio of DTAB and C12-SCDs is between 7:3 and 1:9, the surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixtures is lower than that of the pure components. When the mass ratio is 3:7, the surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture reaches the lowest. For the SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures, the surface tension of the mixture is lower than that of the two pure components at the SDS/C12-SCDs mass ratio between 6:4 and 1:9. At the same time, when the mass ratio is 5:5, the surface tension of the mixture reaches the lowest. Therefore, the synergistic effect of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture is more prominent than the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture.

The synergistic effect of DTAB and C12-SCDs is similar to that of cationic and anionic surfactant mixtures. A strong electrostatic attraction occurs between DTAB and C12-SCDs with different charges, thus significantly improving the surface activity of the mixtures [27]. The photographs of the above mixtures are shown in Fig. 3, and the turbidity measurement results of the above mixtures are shown in Fig. S6. The solution became cloudy at the mass ratios of 7:3, 6:4, 5:5, and 4:6. It can be seen from Fig. S6 that when the mass ratio is 7:3, the turbidity reaches the maximum. As the mass ratio further decreased, the turbidity gradually decreased. Finally, the turbidity is almost zero when the mass ratio is 3:7. According to the above experimental results, it can be seen that when the mass ratio of DTAB and C12-SCDs is 7:3, the system is close to electrical neutrality and evident precipitation is formed. Moreover, when the mass ratio of DTAB and C12-SCDs is 3:7, no precipitate is formed, and the surface activity is the highest. Since both SDS and C12-SCDs are negatively charged, no turbidity is observed, and the turbidity did not change with the mass ratio. Therefore, the hydrophobic interaction between SDS and C12-SCDs benefits the formation of surface adsorption layer and micelles, thereby reducing apparent CMC and apparent γ CMC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the effects of NaCl and CaCl2 on the equilibrium surface tension of the CDs/surfactant mixtures. Our previous report shows these CDs can withstand saturated salt water [26]. With the increase in the concentration of inorganic salts, the surface tension of the mixture shows a trend of first decreasing slightly and then increasing. NaCl had little effect on its surface tension for the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture. Even when the NaCl concentration was as high as 200 g/L, the surface tension only increased by 1–2 mN/m. When the concentration of NaCl is 60 g/L, the surface tension of the mixture reaches the minimum. However, CaCl2 affects surface tension to a greater extent than NaCl. When the CaCl2 concentration is 35 g/L, the surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture reaches the minimum, and then the surface tension first increases and then remains unchanged. Even at CaCl2 concentrations as high as 200 g/L, the surface tensions of mixtures are 31.28 and 29.59 mN/m at a total surfactant/C12-SCDs concentration of 100 mg/L and 200 mg/L, respectively. Compared with DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture, the surface tension of the SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture also shows the same trend. Adding a small number of inorganic salts will compress the double electric layer of CDs and surfactants, making these materials more hydrophobicity [28]. In high brine concentrations, ionic surfactants like DTAB and SDS become insoluble. Although C12-SCDs can tolerate high concentrations of inorganic salts, the precipitation of ionic surfactants will inevitably change the composition of the mixtures and affect the surface tension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH on the equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of pH on the surface tension of the CDs and surfactant mixtures are shown in Fig. 5. The surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture was almost unchanged at a pH below 9. However, when the pH exceeds 9, the surface tension increases gradually, indicating that the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture is resistant to strong acids but not strong alkali environments. Compared with C12-SCDs, the surface tension of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture is less affected by pH. However, the surface tension of SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) gradually increases with the increase of pH, significantly influencing its surface tension. As the pH increases, the negative charge on the C12-SCDs surface gradually increases, so the repulsion between the C12-SCDs and the anionic surfactant SDS becomes strong. As a result, the interaction between C12-SCDs and SDS becomes weak, which weakens the synergistic effect of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of temperature on the equilibrium surface tension of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) and SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixtures were aged at a specific temperature for 48 h to investigate the effects of temperature on the surface activity of the above mixtures. It can be seen from Fig. S7 that the surface tension of the above mixtures increases slightly with the increase in temperature. For example, when the concentration was 50 mg/L, the surface tension of DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixtures increased from 32.17 mN/m to 35.98 mN/m as the temperature increased from 30.0 °C to 120.0 °C; when the concentration was 100 mg/L and 200 mg /L, the surface tension of DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixtures increased from 26.87 mN/m to 28.60 mN/m as the temperature increased to 120.0 °C. For the SDS/C12-SCDs (5:5) mixture, the surface tension increases by about 3 mN/m when the temperature increases to 120.0 °C. The above results show that these mixtures have temperature-resistance properties and can be used in formations with corresponding temperatures to enhance oil recovery.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crude oil-water interfacial properties of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The crude oil-water interfacial tension curves of DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, and the corresponding mixtures are shown in Fig. 6. As the concentration increases, the interfacial tension gradually decreases and tends to equilibrium. The equilibrium crude oil-water interfacial tension (γ eq) of C12-SCDs is 8.95 mN/m, while the γ eq of DTAB and SDS are 5.82 mN/m and 5.31 mN/m, respectively. Unlike the surface activity, the crude oil-water interfacial activity of C12-SCDs is slightly worse than that of SDS and CTAB. It can be seen from Fig. 6 that the crude oil-water interfacial activity of the DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures is better than that of the pure components, and the crude oil-water interfacial tension can be reduced to the extent of 10−1 mN/m. These results indicate that C12-SCDs and surfactants have a synergistic effect in reducing the crude oil-water interfacial tension and have more excellent interfacial activity. However, the apparent CMC of the DTAB/C12-SCDs (3:7) mixture is higher than that of the pure components, while the apparent CMC of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture is close to that of the pure components.

Dynamic interfacial tension curves can reflect the kinetic information of surfactant diffusion, adsorption, and other processes [29]. As shown in Fig. S8, the interfacial tension of crude oil and water gradually decreases with time. With the diffusion and adsorption process of surface-active substances, the interfacial concentration of these substances gradually increases, and the interfacial tension decreases accordingly. In the rapid decrease stage of interfacial tension, the decrease rate of these mixtures is higher than that of the pure components. In addition, the decreased rate of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures is higher than that of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixtures. At the initial stage of interfacial adsorption, the electrostatic repulsion between C12-SCDs and SDS enables C12-SCDs or SDS to rapidly diffuse towards the interface, increasing the adsorption speed of these interface-active substances at the interface, thereby increasing the decrease rate of the interfacial tension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wettability alternation and oil film removal properties of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the underwater contact angle of oil droplets on the glass sheets immersed in different surfactants, CDs, or CDs/surfactant mixtures. The underwater contact angle of oil droplets on the glass sheet immersed in deionized water is 36.2°, indicating that the glass sheet surface is hydrophobic. DTAB, SDS, and C12-SCDs increase the underwater contact angle of oil droplets from 36.2° to 69.6°, 89.3°, and 96.4°, respectively. However, DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures increase the underwater contact angle of oil droplets more effectively. DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures increase the contact angle to 120.6° and 111.6°, respectively. Among them, the increase degree of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixtures is maximal, indicating that the above solution has better wettability alternation performance. CDs and surfactants can form a mixed adsorption layer to promote the wettability alternation performance. DTAB and C12-SCDs have different charges, so the interaction between surfactants and CDs is robust, and a tense surface adsorption layer can be formed on the surface of glass sheets, further reducing the contact angle.

Fig. 8 shows the oil film removal by surfactants, CDs, and the surfactant and CDs mixtures. According to the above photograph, the change curve of the oil film area with time can be obtained. It can be seen from Fig. 8(b) that the oil film removal efficiency follows as DTAB/C12-SCDs mixtures > SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures > SDS > C12-SCDs > DTAB. The above results show that DTAB/ C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures have high oil film removal efficiency related to reducing the oil-water interfacial tension.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decompression and augmented injection of CDs and surfactant mixtures
ce:section.ce:para: The decompression and augmented injection performance of CDs and surfactant mixtures were evaluated by core displacement experiments. First, the mineralized water was injected until the pressure was stable, and then 200 mg/L of DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/C12-CDs mixture, and SDS/C12-CDs mixture were injected into the core, respectively. The variation of injection pressure with injection volume is shown in Fig. 9, and the core properties and depressurization rate are listed in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 9, the injection pressure of mineralized water increases and then drops rapidly with the increase in the injection volume. When the injection volume reaches 4PV, the pressure gradually stabilizes due to the continuous breakthrough of capillary force. Meanwhile, the injection pressures of DTAB, SDS, CDs, DTAB/C12-CDs mixture, and SDS/C12-CDs mixture show the same trend. The depressurization rates of DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/ C12-SCDs mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs mixture are 10.42 %, 11.54 %, 22.91 %, 26.32 %, and 24.75 %, respectively. Therefore, these surfactants and CDs can effectively reduce the injection pressure compared with water flooding. Meanwhile, the DTAB/C12-CDs and SDS/C12-CDs mixtures perform better than single surfactant and C12-SCDs solutions.

The DTAB/C12-CDs and SDS/C12-CDs mixtures can effectively remove the oil film on the core surface, thus improving the channel of injected water. At the same time, the above mixture can be adsorbed on the surface of the hydrophilic core to form a hydrophobic surface, and the injection of water produces a hydrophobic slip effect [30]. Finally, reducing the surface tension of water can also effectively reduce the injection pressure.

Surfactants DTAB and SDS show a synergistic effect in reducing surface tension with C12-SCDs. The synergistic effect of the DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture is more prominent than the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture. Meanwhile, adding inorganic salts, the surface tension of these mixtures shows a trend of first decreasing slightly and then increasing. The DTAB/C12-SCDs mixture resists strong acids but not alkali environments. However, the surface tension of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture gradually increases with the increase in pH. As the pH increases, the interaction between C12-SCDs and SDS becomes weak, which weakens the synergistic effect of the SDS/C12-SCDs mixture.

C12-SCDs and surfactants (DTAB and SDS) also have a synergistic effect in reducing the crude oil-water interfacial tension. Meanwhile, the DTAB/C12-SCDs and SDS/C12-SCDs mixtures have excellent wettability alternation performance. CDs and surfactants can form a mixed adsorption layer to promote the wettability alternation performance. The depressurization rates of DTAB, SDS, C12-SCDs, DTAB/ C12-SCDs mixture, and SDS/C12-SCDs mixture are 10.42 %, 11.54 %, 22.91 %, 26.32 %, and 24.75 %, respectively. The DTAB/C12-CDs and SDS/C12-CDs mixtures can effectively remove the oil film on the core surface, thus improving the channel of injected water. At the same time, the wettability alternation and the surface tension reduction can also effectively reduce the injection pressure.

There is no report on the mixing properties of amphiphilic CDs and surfactants. As a new type of nano-surfactant, it is necessary to study the surface and interfacial properties of amphiphilic CDs and their interaction with surfactants. Future studies should continue to study the microscopic interaction mechanism and the application of nanofluids based on amphiphilic CDs and surfactants in enhancing oil recovery.
Shiwei Wang: Experiments and Drawing; Renling Lu: Experiments; Gang Wang: Experiments; Huarong Liu: Data analysis; Lu Lai: Writing – review & editing, and Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2023.132356.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.
